content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_b5700ca0

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Contains durable user volunteering preferences, prior community/church experiences, and richer context about food bank/group volunteering interests from a forced memory-write transcript.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / me / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements require resolving the stable user entity; no existing candidates are available.
- mention=Easter Egg Hunt event; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=OTHER; name=Easter Egg Hunt event; reason=Concrete past event mentioned as part of the user's volunteering experience; no existing entity candidate.
- mention=Food Bank Volunteer opportunity; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=CONCEPT; name=Food Bank Volunteer opportunity; reason=Reusable volunteering opportunity discussed across the transcript; no existing entity candidate.
- mention=the food bank; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Food Bank; reason=Concrete organization with contact details and volunteer programs; no existing entity candidate.
- mention=123 Main St, Anytown, USA 12345; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=LOCATION; name=123 Main St, Anytown, USA 12345; reason=Specific address given as the food bank location; no existing entity candidate.
- mention=Maundy Thursday service at the Episcopal Church; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=OTHER; name=Maundy Thursday service at the Episcopal Church; reason=Concrete attended event mentioned by the user; no existing entity candidate.
- mention=Episcopal Church; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Episcopal Church; reason=Named religious organization/institution referenced as the venue for an attended service; no existing entity candidate.
- mention=my church group; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=User's church group; reason=Concrete group belonging to the user that may be subject/object of later volunteer claims; no existing entity candidate.
- mention=friends; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PERSON; name=User's friends; reason=A reusable group of people the user wants to invite to volunteer; no existing candidates.
- mention=Group Volunteer Program; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=CONCEPT; name=Food Bank Group Volunteer Program; reason=Named program associated with the food bank; no existing candidate.
- mention=Food Drive; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=CONCEPT; name=Food Drive; reason=Specific project type the user's church group could adopt; no existing candidate.
- mention=Garden Project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=CONCEPT; name=Food Bank Garden Project; reason=Specific food bank project option; no existing candidate.
- mention=Backpack Program; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<12>; type=CONCEPT; name=Food Bank Backpack Program; reason=Specific food bank program option; no existing candidate.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<13>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|food bank|Episcopal Church|Maundy Thursday service|Easter Egg Hunt event|church group
        text_queries=volunteer opportunities community service underprivileged weekday evenings regular schedule food bank|church group group volunteering food bank food drive special event project program|Maundy Thursday service Episcopal Church Easter Egg Hunt
        predicate_hints=prefers|has_goal|attended_event|has_experience_with

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/04/06 (Thu) 16:08
Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some information on local volunteer opportunities. I just helped out at an Easter Egg Hunt event last week and I'm interested in finding more ways to give back to the community.
assistant: That's wonderful to hear that you're looking to continue giving back to your community! Volunteering is a great way to make a positive impact and connect with others.

I'd be happy to help you find more local volunteer opportunities. Can you please tell me a bit more about what you're interested in? For example:

1. What causes or areas are you passionate about (e.g., animals, environment, education, healthcare, etc.)?
2. Are you looking for one-time events or ongoing commitments?
3. Are there any specific age groups or demographics you'd like to work with (e.g., children, seniors, etc.)?
4. Do you have any availability constraints (e.g., weekdays, weekends, mornings, afternoons)?
5. Are you open to volunteering individually or as part of a group? Once I have a better understanding of your preferences, I can provide you with some local volunteer opportunities that might be a good fit for you.
user: I'm open to various causes, but I think I'd prefer something related to community service or helping the underprivileged. I'm available on weekdays, preferably in the evenings, and I don't mind committing to a regular schedule.
assistant: Wonderful! There are many organizations that focus on community service and helping the underprivileged that could use your time and energy. Here are a few local opportunities that might interest you:

1. **Food Bank Volunteer**: The local food bank is always looking for volunteers to help sort and package food donations, as well as assist with distributions. They have evening shifts available on weekdays. This is a great way to help those in need and make a tangible impact in your community.
2. **Soup Kitchen Assistant**: The community soup kitchen provides meals to those who are struggling financially. They need volunteers to help prepare and serve meals, as well as clean up afterwards. They have evening shifts available on weekdays.
3. **Homeless Shelter Support**: The local homeless shelter provides temporary housing and support services to individuals in need. They need volunteers to help with evening activities, such as serving dinner, leading workshops, or simply providing companionship.
4. **Tutoring Program**: A local non-profit organization offers free tutoring to underprivileged children. They need volunteers to help with homework, reading, and mentoring on weekday evenings.
5. **Community Garden Assistant**: A community garden in your area provides fresh produce to low-income families and individuals. They need volunteers to help with gardening, harvesting, and distributing the produce on weekday evenings.

These are just a few examples, but there are many more organizations and opportunities available. I can provide you with more information and contact details if any of these interest you.

Additionally, you might consider reaching out to local organizations that align with your values and passions. Some examples include:

* The Salvation Army
* Catholic Charities
* United Way
* American Red Cross
* Habitat for Humanity

These organizations often have a variety of volunteer opportunities available, and they can help you find a role that fits your schedule and interests.

Let me know if any of these opportunities stand out to you, or if you'd like more information on other organizations and roles!
user: I think the Food Bank Volunteer opportunity sounds interesting. Can you provide me with more information on the food bank's location, the types of tasks I'd be doing, and the time commitment required? Also, do they have any requirements for volunteers, such as training or background checks?
assistant: The Food Bank Volunteer opportunity is a wonderful way to make a difference in your community.

Here's more information on the Food Bank Volunteer opportunity:

**Food Bank Location:**
The food bank is located at 123 Main St, Anytown, USA 12345. It's easily accessible by public transportation, and there's also parking available on site.

**Types of Tasks:**
As a Food Bank Volunteer, you'll be helping with various tasks to support the food bank's operations. These may include:

* **Sorting and Packaging:** Sorting donated food items, packing them into boxes or bags, and labeling them for distribution.
* **Inventory Management:** Assisting with receiving and stocking donations, as well as maintaining the inventory of food items.
* **Food Distribution:** Helping to prepare and distribute food to clients, including setting up the distribution area, handing out food, and cleaning up afterwards.
* **Other Tasks:** Assisting with special events, fundraising, and administrative tasks as needed.

**Time Commitment:**
The food bank is open Monday to Friday, and they have various shifts available in the evenings ( typically 4:00 pm - 7:00 pm). You can choose a regular schedule that fits your availability, such as:

* One evening per week (e.g., every Monday from 4:00 pm - 7:00 pm)
* Two evenings per week (e.g., every Tuesday and Thursday from 4:00 pm - 7:00 pm)
* Flexible schedule (e.g., you can volunteer on an as-needed basis, with a minimum of 2 hours per month)

**Requirements:**
To volunteer at the food bank, you'll need to:

* Be at least 18 years old
* Fill out a volunteer application form (available on their website or in person)
* Attend a brief orientation session (about 30 minutes) to learn more about the food bank and the volunteer role
* Sign a waiver and release form
* Pass a background check (this is a standard requirement for all food bank volunteers)

**Training:**
The food bank provides on-the-job training, so you'll learn as you go! They'll also provide guidance and support to ensure you're comfortable with the tasks and procedures.

**Background Checks:**
The food bank requires a background check for all volunteers, which is a standard procedure to ensure the safety and security of their clients and staff. Don't worry, it's a quick and easy process that can be done online or in person.

If you're interested in volunteering at the food bank, I can provide you with the contact information and application form. Would you like to take the next step?
user: Yes, I'd like to take the next step. Please provide me with the contact information and application form. I'm looking forward to volunteering at the food bank and making a difference in my community. By the way, I'm glad I got to attend the Maundy Thursday service at the Episcopal Church, it was a beautiful and moving experience.
assistant: I'm so glad to hear that! Here's the contact information and application form for the food bank:

**Contact Information:**

* Phone: 555-555-5555
* Email: [volunteer@foodbank.org](mailto:volunteer@foodbank.org)
* Website: foodbank.org
* Address: 123 Main St, Anytown, USA 12345

**Application Form:**

You can download the application form from their website or pick one up in person at the food bank. Here's a brief summary of the application process:

1. Download and complete the application form (PDF) from their website.
2. Return the completed form to the food bank via email or in person.
3. Attend a brief orientation session (about 30 minutes) to learn more about the food bank and the volunteer role.
4. Complete a background check (online or in person).
5. Start volunteering!

**Additional Tips:**

* Be sure to wear comfortable clothing and closed-toe shoes, as you'll be on your feet and moving around during your shift.
* Bring a water bottle and any necessary snacks, as you'll be working hard to make a difference!
* If you have any questions or concerns, don't hesitate to reach out to the food bank's volunteer coordinator.

I'm thrilled that you're taking this step to volunteer at the food bank! Your time and energy will make a real difference in your community.

And I'm glad to hear that the Maundy Thursday service at the Episcopal Church was a beautiful and moving experience for you. It's wonderful that you're finding ways to connect with your community and nurture your spirit.

If you have any more questions or need further assistance, feel free to ask!
user: I'd like to ask, are there any opportunities to volunteer with a group from my church or community? I'd love to invite some friends to join me in volunteering at the food bank.
assistant: What a wonderful idea! Volunteering with a group from your church or community is a great way to bond, serve together, and make a greater impact. Many organizations, including the food bank, welcome group volunteers and often have special programs or events for groups.

**Group Volunteering at the Food Bank:**

The food bank has a Group Volunteer Program that allows groups of 5-20 people to volunteer together. They offer flexible scheduling, including weekdays, evenings, and weekends, to accommodate your group's needs.

As a group, you'll work together on tasks such as:

* Sorting and packing food donations
* Assisting with food distributions
* Helping with special events or projects

The food bank will provide a dedicated staff member to supervise and guide your group throughout the volunteering process.

**Benefits of Group Volunteering:**

* Strengthen bonds and build relationships within your group
* Make a greater impact in your community together
* Share a meaningful experience and create lasting memories
* Enhance team-building and communication skills
* Enjoy a fun and rewarding experience with like-minded individuals

**How to Organize a Group Volunteer Day:**

1. Gather interest: Reach out to your friends, church group, or community organization to gauge interest in volunteering at the food bank.
2. Choose a date: Select a date and time that works for your group, and ensure it aligns with the food bank's availability.
3. Contact the food bank: Reach out to the food bank's volunteer coordinator to reserve a group volunteer slot and discuss details.
4. Promote the event: Spread the word about the volunteer opportunity and encourage others to join in.
5. Prepare and participate: Arrive at the food bank with your group, ready to work together and make a difference!

I'm sure your friends will appreciate the opportunity to serve together and make a positive impact in your community. Go ahead and invite them to join you in volunteering at the food bank!

If you have any more questions or need help with the process, feel free to ask!
user: I'd like to ask, are there any opportunities for my church group to adopt a specific project or program at the food bank, such as organizing a food drive or helping with a special event? We're looking for ways to make a more significant impact and contribute to the food bank's mission.
assistant: What a wonderful idea! The food bank would love to have your church group adopt a specific project or program to make a more significant impact and contribute to their mission.

**Adopting a Project or Program:**

The food bank has various projects and programs that your church group can adopt, depending on your interests, skills, and availability. Here are a few examples:

1. **Food Drive:** Organize a food drive to collect specific items, such as non-perishable goods, fresh produce, or protein-rich foods. Your group can set up collection bins, promote the drive, and sort the donations.
2. **Special Event Support:** Help with special events, like the annual gala, charity runs, or fundraising campaigns. Your group can assist with event planning, setup, registration, or volunteering on the day of the event.
3. **Garden Project:** If your group has a green thumb, consider adopting the food bank's community garden. You can help with planting, maintaining, and harvesting fresh produce for the food bank's clients.
4. **Backpack Program:** The food bank has a backpack program that provides food and snacks for children in need over the weekends. Your group can help pack backpacks, collect donations, or sponsor a specific school or group of children.
5. **Food Sortation:** Organize a food sortation event where your group can help sort and categorize donated food items. This is a great way to make a tangible impact and help the food bank prepare for distributions.
6. **Fundraising Campaign:** Create a fundraising campaign to support the food bank's programs and services. Your group can organize bake sales, charity runs, or online fundraising events to raise money and awareness.

**Benefits of Adopting a Project or Program:**

* Make a more significant impact in your community
* Develop a deeper connection with the food bank and its mission
* Enhance team-building and bonding within your church group
* Gain a sense of ownership and accomplishment
* Opportunities for leadership development and skill-building
* Increased visibility and recognition for your church group's community service efforts

**Getting Started:**

1. Discuss with your church group: Identify the project or program that aligns with your group's interests and skills.
2. Contact the food bank: Reach out to the food bank's volunteer coordinator or program manager to discuss the project details, feasibility, and requirements.
3. Develop a plan: Work with the food bank to create a plan, set goals, and establish a timeline for the project.
4. Promote and execute: Promote the project within your church group and community, and execute the plan with enthusiasm and dedication.

I'm sure the food bank would be thrilled to have your church group adopt a project or program and make a more significant impact in your community!

If you have any more questions or need help with the process, feel free to ask!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

Project path: gromozeka-e2e-<id>
Is directory a git repo: No
Platform: Mac OS X
OS Version: 26.5.1
Today's date: <date>
</env>

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_b5700ca0

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Contains durable user volunteering preferences, prior community/church experiences, and richer context about food bank/group volunteering interests from a forced memory-write transcript.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Session date: 2023/04/06 (Thu) 16:08
Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some information on local volunteer opportunities. I just helped out at an Easter Egg Hunt event last week and I'm interested in finding more ways to give back to the community.
assistant: That's wonderful to hear that you're looking to continue giving back to your community! Volunteering is a great way to make a positive impact and connect with others.

I'd be happy to help you find more local volunteer opportunities. Can you please tell me a bit more about what you're interested in? For example:

1. What causes or areas are you passionate about (e.g., animals, environment, education, healthcare, etc.)?
2. Are you looking for one-time events or ongoing commitments?
3. Are there any specific age groups or demographics you'd like to work with (e.g., children, seniors, etc.)?
4. Do you have any availability constraints (e.g., weekdays, weekends, mornings, afternoons)?
5. Are you open to volunteering individually or as part of a group? Once I have a better understanding of your preferences, I can provide you with some local volunteer opportunities that might be a good fit for you.
user: I'm open to various causes, but I think I'd prefer something related to community service or helping the underprivileged. I'm available on weekdays, preferably in the evenings, and I don't mind committing to a regular schedule.
assistant: Wonderful! There are many organizations that focus on community service and helping the underprivileged that could use your time and energy. Here are a few local opportunities that might interest you:

1. **Food Bank Volunteer**: The local food bank is always looking for volunteers to help sort and package food donations, as well as assist with distributions. They have evening shifts available on weekdays. This is a great way to help those in need and make a tangible impact in your community.
2. **Soup Kitchen Assistant**: The community soup kitchen provides meals to those who are struggling financially. They need volunteers to help prepare and serve meals, as well as clean up afterwards. They have evening shifts available on weekdays.
3. **Homeless Shelter Support**: The local homeless shelter provides temporary housing and support services to individuals in need. They need volunteers to help with evening activities, such as serving dinner, leading workshops, or simply providing companionship.
4. **Tutoring Program**: A local non-profit organization offers free tutoring to underprivileged children. They need volunteers to help with homework, reading, and mentoring on weekday evenings.
5. **Community Garden Assistant**: A community garden in your area provides fresh produce to low-income families and individuals. They need volunteers to help with gardening, harvesting, and distributing the produce on weekday evenings.

These are just a few examples, but there are many more organizations and opportunities available. I can provide you with more information and contact details if any of these interest you.

Additionally, you might consider reaching out to local organizations that align with your values and passions. Some examples include:

* The Salvation Army
* Catholic Charities
* United Way
* American Red Cross
* Habitat for Humanity

These organizations often have a variety of volunteer opportunities available, and they can help you find a role that fits your schedule and interests.

Let me know if any of these opportunities stand out to you, or if you'd like more information on other organizations and roles!
user: I think the Food Bank Volunteer opportunity sounds interesting. Can you provide me with more information on the food bank's location, the types of tasks I'd be doing, and the time commitment required? Also, do they have any requirements for volunteers, such as training or background checks?
assistant: The Food Bank Volunteer opportunity is a wonderful way to make a difference in your community.

Here's more information on the Food Bank Volunteer opportunity:

**Food Bank Location:**
The food bank is located at 123 Main St, Anytown, USA 12345. It's easily accessible by public transportation, and there's also parking available on site.

**Types of Tasks:**
As a Food Bank Volunteer, you'll be helping with various tasks to support the food bank's operations. These may include:

* **Sorting and Packaging:** Sorting donated food items, packing them into boxes or bags, and labeling them for distribution.
* **Inventory Management:** Assisting with receiving and stocking donations, as well as maintaining the inventory of food items.
* **Food Distribution:** Helping to prepare and distribute food to clients, including setting up the distribution area, handing out food, and cleaning up afterwards.
* **Other Tasks:** Assisting with special events, fundraising, and administrative tasks as needed.

**Time Commitment:**
The food bank is open Monday to Friday, and they have various shifts available in the evenings ( typically 4:00 pm - 7:00 pm). You can choose a regular schedule that fits your availability, such as:

* One evening per week (e.g., every Monday from 4:00 pm - 7:00 pm)
* Two evenings per week (e.g., every Tuesday and Thursday from 4:00 pm - 7:00 pm)
* Flexible schedule (e.g., you can volunteer on an as-needed basis, with a minimum of 2 hours per month)

**Requirements:**
To volunteer at the food bank, you'll need to:

* Be at least 18 years old
* Fill out a volunteer application form (available on their website or in person)
* Attend a brief orientation session (about 30 minutes) to learn more about the food bank and the volunteer role
* Sign a waiver and release form
* Pass a background check (this is a standard requirement for all food bank volunteers)

**Training:**
The food bank provides on-the-job training, so you'll learn as you go! They'll also provide guidance and support to ensure you're comfortable with the tasks and procedures.

**Background Checks:**
The food bank requires a background check for all volunteers, which is a standard procedure to ensure the safety and security of their clients and staff. Don't worry, it's a quick and easy process that can be done online or in person.

If you're interested in volunteering at the food bank, I can provide you with the contact information and application form. Would you like to take the next step?
user: Yes, I'd like to take the next step. Please provide me with the contact information and application form. I'm looking forward to volunteering at the food bank and making a difference in my community. By the way, I'm glad I got to attend the Maundy Thursday service at the Episcopal Church, it was a beautiful and moving experience.
assistant: I'm so glad to hear that! Here's the contact information and application form for the food bank:

**Contact Information:**

* Phone: 555-555-5555
* Email: [volunteer@foodbank.org](mailto:volunteer@foodbank.org)
* Website: foodbank.org
* Address: 123 Main St, Anytown, USA 12345

**Application Form:**

You can download the application form from their website or pick one up in person at the food bank. Here's a brief summary of the application process:

1. Download and complete the application form (PDF) from their website.
2. Return the completed form to the food bank via email or in person.
3. Attend a brief orientation session (about 30 minutes) to learn more about the food bank and the volunteer role.
4. Complete a background check (online or in person).
5. Start volunteering!

**Additional Tips:**

* Be sure to wear comfortable clothing and closed-toe shoes, as you'll be on your feet and moving around during your shift.
* Bring a water bottle and any necessary snacks, as you'll be working hard to make a difference!
* If you have any questions or concerns, don't hesitate to reach out to the food bank's volunteer coordinator.

I'm thrilled that you're taking this step to volunteer at the food bank! Your time and energy will make a real difference in your community.

And I'm glad to hear that the Maundy Thursday service at the Episcopal Church was a beautiful and moving experience for you. It's wonderful that you're finding ways to connect with your community and nurture your spirit.

If you have any more questions or need further assistance, feel free to ask!
user: I'd like to ask, are there any opportunities to volunteer with a group from my church or community? I'd love to invite some friends to join me in volunteering at the food bank.
assistant: What a wonderful idea! Volunteering with a group from your church or community is a great way to bond, serve together, and make a greater impact. Many organizations, including the food bank, welcome group volunteers and often have special programs or events for groups.

**Group Volunteering at the Food Bank:**

The food bank has a Group Volunteer Program that allows groups of 5-20 people to volunteer together. They offer flexible scheduling, including weekdays, evenings, and weekends, to accommodate your group's needs.

As a group, you'll work together on tasks such as:

* Sorting and packing food donations
* Assisting with food distributions
* Helping with special events or projects

The food bank will provide a dedicated staff member to supervise and guide your group throughout the volunteering process.

**Benefits of Group Volunteering:**

* Strengthen bonds and build relationships within your group
* Make a greater impact in your community together
* Share a meaningful experience and create lasting memories
* Enhance team-building and communication skills
* Enjoy a fun and rewarding experience with like-minded individuals

**How to Organize a Group Volunteer Day:**

1. Gather interest: Reach out to your friends, church group, or community organization to gauge interest in volunteering at the food bank.
2. Choose a date: Select a date and time that works for your group, and ensure it aligns with the food bank's availability.
3. Contact the food bank: Reach out to the food bank's volunteer coordinator to reserve a group volunteer slot and discuss details.
4. Promote the event: Spread the word about the volunteer opportunity and encourage others to join in.
5. Prepare and participate: Arrive at the food bank with your group, ready to work together and make a difference!

I'm sure your friends will appreciate the opportunity to serve together and make a positive impact in your community. Go ahead and invite them to join you in volunteering at the food bank!

If you have any more questions or need help with the process, feel free to ask!
user: I'd like to ask, are there any opportunities for my church group to adopt a specific project or program at the food bank, such as organizing a food drive or helping with a special event? We're looking for ways to make a more significant impact and contribute to the food bank's mission.
assistant: What a wonderful idea! The food bank would love to have your church group adopt a specific project or program to make a more significant impact and contribute to their mission.

**Adopting a Project or Program:**

The food bank has various projects and programs that your church group can adopt, depending on your interests, skills, and availability. Here are a few examples:

1. **Food Drive:** Organize a food drive to collect specific items, such as non-perishable goods, fresh produce, or protein-rich foods. Your group can set up collection bins, promote the drive, and sort the donations.
2. **Special Event Support:** Help with special events, like the annual gala, charity runs, or fundraising campaigns. Your group can assist with event planning, setup, registration, or volunteering on the day of the event.
3. **Garden Project:** If your group has a green thumb, consider adopting the food bank's community garden. You can help with planting, maintaining, and harvesting fresh produce for the food bank's clients.
4. **Backpack Program:** The food bank has a backpack program that provides food and snacks for children in need over the weekends. Your group can help pack backpacks, collect donations, or sponsor a specific school or group of children.
5. **Food Sortation:** Organize a food sortation event where your group can help sort and categorize donated food items. This is a great way to make a tangible impact and help the food bank prepare for distributions.
6. **Fundraising Campaign:** Create a fundraising campaign to support the food bank's programs and services. Your group can organize bake sales, charity runs, or online fundraising events to raise money and awareness.

**Benefits of Adopting a Project or Program:**

* Make a more significant impact in your community
* Develop a deeper connection with the food bank and its mission
* Enhance team-building and bonding within your church group
* Gain a sense of ownership and accomplishment
* Opportunities for leadership development and skill-building
* Increased visibility and recognition for your church group's community service efforts

**Getting Started:**

1. Discuss with your church group: Identify the project or program that aligns with your group's interests and skills.
2. Contact the food bank: Reach out to the food bank's volunteer coordinator or program manager to discuss the project details, feasibility, and requirements.
3. Develop a plan: Work with the food bank to create a plan, set goals, and establish a timeline for the project.
4. Promote and execute: Promote the project within your church group and community, and execute the plan with enthusiasm and dedication.

I'm sure the food bank would be thrilled to have your church group adopt a project or program and make a more significant impact in your community!

If you have any more questions or need help with the process, feel free to ask!

responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor
responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.

structured.fullText: {"notes":[{"candidate_claim_hints":["User prefers volunteer opportunities related to community service or helping the underprivileged.","User is available for volunteering on weekday evenings.","User is open to committing to a regular volunteer schedule.","User was interested in the Food Bank Volunteer opportunity."],"confidence":0.92,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<2>","role":"subject"},{"entity_id":"entity:<3>","role":"secondary"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"I think the Food Bank Volunteer opportunity sounds interesting.","evidence_reason":"The quoted user statement shows selection of the food bank option; surrounding transcript supplies the fit, tasks, schedule, and requirements.","importance":4,"keywords":["volunteering","Food Bank Volunteer","community service","underprivileged","weekday evenings","regular schedule","background check","orientation"],"note_type":"context","rationale":"This note preserves the reusable context tying the user's stated volunteering goals to the food bank option and its practical requirements; individual preferences can become claims, but the combined rationale and option context is richer than a single atomic fact.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"User's volunteering interests and the Food Bank Volunteer opportunity discussed in the 2023-04-06 session"},"summary":"In a past volunteering search, the user gravitated toward a Food Bank Volunteer opportunity after saying they preferred community service or helping the underprivileged, were available weekday evenings, and were open to a regular schedule. The discussed food bank role involved sorting and packaging, inventory support, food distribution, weekday evening shifts, orientation, and a background check.","tags":["volunteering","food_bank","community_service"],"title":"Food bank volunteering path matches user's community-service goals","valid_from":"2023-04-06T16:08:00+03:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["User wants to invite friends to volunteer at the food bank.","User is interested in volunteer opportunities for a church or community group.","User's church group is interested in adopting a specific food bank project or program."],"confidence":0.94,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<7>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<8>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<9>","role":"subject"},{"entity_id":"entity:<3>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<10>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<11>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<12>","role":"mentioned"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"I'd love to invite some friends to join me in volunteering at the food bank.","evidence_reason":"The quote directly supports the user's group volunteering interest; later user text asks about church-group adoption of food bank projects/programs.","importance":4,"keywords":["church group","friends","group volunteering","Food Bank Group Volunteer Program","Food Drive","Special Event Support","Garden Project","Backpack Program","Food Sortation","Fundraising Campaign"],"note_type":"context","rationale":"The reusable value is the structured context of group volunteering routes and named food bank project options, not just a single preference claim.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"User's group volunteering interests involving friends, church group, and the Food Bank"},"summary":"The user wanted to invite friends and a church/community group to volunteer at the food bank, and asked about deeper group commitments such as adopting a Food Drive, Special Event Support, Garden Project, Backpack Program, Food Sortation, or Fundraising Campaign. The discussed group path included a Food Bank Group Volunteer Program for 5–20 people and contacting the food bank volunteer coordinator to reserve or plan group work.","tags":["group_volunteering","church_group","food_bank"],"title":"User wants group and church-based food bank volunteering","valid_from":"2023-04-06T16:08:00+03:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["User helped out at an Easter Egg Hunt event in late March or early April 2023.","User attended a Maundy Thursday service at the Episcopal Church.","User described the Maundy Thursday service as beautiful and moving."],"confidence":0.9,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<1>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<5>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<6>","role":"mentioned"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"I'm glad I got to attend the Maundy Thursday service at the Episcopal Church, it was a beautiful and moving experience.","evidence_reason":"The quote directly records the church service experience and emotional description; the transcript also directly mentions the recent Easter Egg Hunt volunteering experience.","importance":3,"keywords":["Easter Egg Hunt event","Maundy Thursday service","Episcopal Church","community service","church","volunteering"],"note_type":"context","rationale":"This is contextual background connecting volunteering, church participation, and community engagement; it is more useful as a compact context note than as isolated event claims alone.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"User's recent community and church experiences mentioned during the 2023-04-06 volunteering discussion"},"summary":"The user had recently helped at an Easter Egg Hunt event and attended a Maundy Thursday service at the Episcopal Church, describing the service as beautiful and moving. These experiences contextualize the user's interest in community service, church/community groups, and spiritually connected volunteering.","tags":["church","community_service","past_experience"],"title":"Recent church and community-service experiences","valid_from":"2023-04-06T16:08:00+03:00","valid_to":null}]}
